subtopics.subtopic_description: Educational background or training
notes: Holds a Master’s Degree in a health sciences or data-related field (e.g., Biostatistics, Clinical Data Management, Public Health, or Bioinformatics)
Completed undergraduate studies in a related discipline such as Life Sciences, Statistics, or Computer Science
Participated in specialized coursework on data analysis, clinical research, and regulatory compliance
Attended workshops on SAS and R programming as part of graduate studies

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific job title and role description
notes: Current job title: Clinical Data Manager
Responsible for managing and processing clinical trial data
Regularly generates FDA-required datasets, tables, listings, and figures
Acts as a liaison between statistical, medical, and regulatory teams for clinical projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Current industry or sector (e.g., tech, finance, manufacturing)
notes: Works in the pharmaceutical and clinical research industry
Operates within the health and life sciences sector
Focuses on clinical trials and regulatory submissions for drug development

subtopics.subtopic_description: Company size and environment
notes: Employed at a mid-sized Contract Research Organization (CRO) or pharmaceutical company
Works in a collaborative office environment with cross-functional teams
Team size for data management is typically between 10-30 members

subtopics.subtopic_description: Type of business or market segment
notes: Company operates in contract-based clinical research and data management services
Primary clients include pharmaceutical companies conducting clinical trials
Market segment focuses on regulatory-compliant data processing for drug approval

subtopics.subtopic_description: Duration/years of experience in current role
notes: Has 1-2 years of experience as a Clinical Data Manager
Started current role soon after completing a Master's degree
Experience primarily focused on early career, entry-to-mid level tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Professional seniority or career level
notes: Holds an entry-level to junior professional position
Not in a supervisory or managerial role
Reports to a senior data manager or project lead

subtopics.subtopic_description: Primary job responsibilities and regular daily tasks
notes: Generates datasets, tables, listings, and figures required by the FDA for clinical trials
Programs in SAS and R to clean, validate, and analyze clinical data
Communicates with cross-functional departments (e.g., biostatistics, clinical operations, regulatory affairs)
Documents data management processes for compliance and audit purposes
Tracks data queries and resolves discrepancies with clinical project teams

subtopics.subtopic_description: Approximate proportion of time spent on core activities
notes: Spends approximately 60-70% of work time on programming and data processing tasks
Devotes 20% of time to cross-functional communication and meetings
Allocates 10-15% of time to documentation and compliance activities
A small proportion (<5%) is spent on training or learning new tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of autonomy and scope of decision-making in the role
notes: Works under supervision of a senior manager or project lead
Has autonomy in day-to-day programming and basic data management decisions
Major process or methodological decisions require sign-off from senior staff
Can suggest improvements but does not set overall departmental policy

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that feel easiest or most natural to perform
notes: Running standard SAS or R scripts to generate routine tables and listings
Cleaning data using predefined validation rules
Responding to routine data queries with template responses
Documenting standard data management procedures

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks perceived as most challenging or complex
notes: Debugging complex programming errors in SAS or R, especially when they involve integration between datasets
Designing new data structures or custom statistical outputs for novel clinical studies
Coordinating data requirements across multiple departments with conflicting priorities
Ensuring compliance with evolving FDA guidance and regulatory requirements

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are repetitive, data-heavy, or suitable for automation
notes: Routine generation of summary tables and listings for each trial cycle
Bulk data cleaning and validation steps that follow standard logic
Preparing standard reports for internal review
Reformatting datasets to meet regulatory submission templates

subtopics.subtopic_description: Tasks that are most enjoyable or engaging versus those that feel boring or tedious
notes: Enjoys collaborating with cross-functional teams to solve unique data challenges
Finds programming new analytical solutions engaging
Repetitive data cleaning and documentation tasks are less interesting
Large-scale data formatting or error correction can feel tedious

subtopics.subtopic_description: Common pain points or inefficiencies in completing tasks
notes: Manual data entry or correction is time-consuming and error-prone
Integration issues between SAS, R, and other clinical data systems
Lack of standardized templates for certain regulatory documents
Occasional communication breakdowns between departments slow project timelines

subtopics.subtopic_description: How enjoyment, skill level, and productivity relate to one another
notes: Feels most productive and engaged when solving novel problems or debugging challenging code
Enjoyment increases when learning new techniques or applying automation to repetitive tasks
Repetitive tasks, though easy, are less enjoyable and are performed more quickly but less enthusiastically
Skill development in programming directly correlates with increased job satisfaction

subtopics.subtopic_description: Attitude towards learning new technologies and tools
notes: Generally enthusiastic about adopting new technologies that improve workflow
Open to experimenting with emerging data analysis tools and AI platforms
Enjoys keeping up with advancements in clinical data management

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived adaptability to new software/methods
notes: Considers self highly adaptable to new software due to regular exposure in current role
Can quickly learn new programming packages or updates in SAS/R
Has successfully integrated LLM-based tools into daily work processes

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness to invest time in tech training
notes: Willing to dedicate personal and work time to tech training if it has clear benefits
Participates in webinars, online courses, or internal workshops to upskill
Prefers hands-on learning experiences over purely theoretical training

subtopics.subtopic_description: Motivations or barriers to learning new tech (e.g., workload, relevance)
notes: Motivated by desire to automate tedious tasks and increase efficiency
Barriers include high workload and project deadlines limiting time for training
Learning is prioritized when technology is directly relevant to current projects

subtopics.subtopic_description: Influence of peers or management on willingness to adopt new tools
notes: Peers often share recommendations for new tools, influencing adoption
Management encouragement increases willingness to trial new software
Observes industry trends and best practices discussed within the team

topic: Primary Tools and Technologies Used in Work

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific software, platforms, or systems used daily
notes: Uses SAS for statistical programming and data management tasks
Applies R for data analysis, visualization, and generating reports
Employs LLM-based tools (e.g., ChatGPT, Bard) to assist with code debugging and communication
Works with Microsoft Office Suite for documentation and presentations

subtopics.subtopic_description: Essential non-AI tools for workflow
notes: Relies on Excel for data review and preliminary data manipulation
Uses email (e.g., Outlook) for project communication
Utilizes internal document management systems for version control and compliance
Employs project management software for tracking timelines and deliverables

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with industry-standard technologies
notes: Proficient in SAS and R, both considered industry standards in clinical data management
Familiar with CDISC SDTM and ADaM data standards for regulatory submissions
Understands basic use of electronic data capture (EDC) systems, such as Medidata Rave or REDCap
Knowledgeable about 21 CFR Part 11 compliance for electronic records

subtopics.subtopic_description: Interoperability or integration issues between tools
notes: Occasionally encounters compatibility issues when transferring data between SAS, R, and Excel
Formatting inconsistencies can arise when importing/exporting data for regulatory submissions
Limited direct integration between LLM tools and legacy clinical data management systems

subtopics.subtopic_description: Familiarity with fundamental AI/ML concepts and terminology
notes: Understands basic concepts such as machine learning, natural language processing, and large language models
Recognizes key AI terminology like prompt engineering, model fine-tuning, and generative AI
Follows developments in AI relevant to data analysis and automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Names of specific AI software/platforms currently used in work
notes: Uses ChatGPT (OpenAI) for improving email drafts and debugging code
Has experimented with Bard (Google) for similar tasks
May use Copilot (Microsoft) for assistance within Office applications
Utilizes AI-based code suggestion plugins in coding environments

subtopics.subtopic_description: Frequency and purpose of AI tool application (specific use cases)
notes: Applies LLMs daily for code review and debugging in SAS and R
Uses AI to generate and proofread professional emails and project updates
Occasionally leverages AI tools to summarize regulatory guidance or documentation
Explores AI suggestions when stuck on complex programming issues

subtopics.subtopic_description: Specific examples of AI success and failure experiences (lessons learned)
notes: Successfully used ChatGPT to resolve a persistent bug in a clinical data summary script
Found LLMs effective for quickly generating draft responses to standard data queries
Experienced occasional AI-generated code that was syntactically correct but contextually inappropriate for clinical data standards
Learned to always verify AI outputs before implementation, especially for regulatory submissions

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of organizational training or peer resources for AI use
notes: Informal peer-to-peer sharing of AI tool tips within the team
No formal company-wide training yet, but growing interest in structured AI upskilling
Occasional lunch-and-learn sessions or knowledge sharing on AI best practices

topic: AI Interaction Style and Workflow Change

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preferred mode of interaction: independent versus step-by-step collaboration
notes: Prefers step-by-step collaboration when tackling complex programming or data issues
Often interacts independently with AI tools for routine tasks like email drafting
Combines both approaches depending on task complexity and urgency

subtopics.subtopic_description: Style of human-AI teaming (e.g., advisor, assistant, co-worker)
notes: Views AI as a digital assistant, providing suggestions and troubleshooting support
Uses AI as an advisor for brainstorming alternative coding or documentation approaches
Treats AI as a helpful co-worker for repetitive or well-defined tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Willingness and openness to adopting new AI-driven workflows
notes: Highly open to new AI-driven workflows if they save time or reduce errors
Willing to advocate for broader AI adoption within the team
Regularly experiments with new AI features as they become available

subtopics.subtopic_description: Preference for conversational vs. command-based interfaces (communication dynamics)
notes: Prefers conversational interfaces for brainstorming, email drafting, and code explanations
Uses command-based prompts for straightforward code generation or data processing tasks
Values flexibility to switch between conversational and command-based styles as needed

subtopics.subtopic_description: Extent to which tasks rely on specialized, tacit domain knowledge
notes: Many tasks require understanding of clinical trial protocols and FDA data standards
Tacit knowledge is needed to interpret regulatory guidelines and ensure compliance
Programming tasks can be performed by AI, but domain-specific context is critical for quality

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of trust in AI outputs for work tasks and critical decisions
notes: Trusts AI outputs for routine coding and communication tasks, provided human review is possible
Exercises caution and performs manual validation for critical or regulatory-sensitive outputs
Does not fully delegate high-stakes decisions to AI without oversight

subtopics.subtopic_description: Ideal balance of human effort and AI automation for specific tasks
notes: Prefers AI automation for repetitive, well-defined tasks like formatting tables or drafting emails
Believes human oversight is essential for interpreting results and ensuring regulatory compliance
Sees value in hybrid workflows where AI handles grunt work and humans provide final review

subtopics.subtopic_description: Conditions under which high automation is acceptable or threatening
notes: Accepts high automation when tasks are standardized and impact is low-risk
Finds high automation threatening if it impinges on quality assurance or regulatory compliance
Requires clear override mechanisms and transparency for critical clinical data tasks

topic: AI Impact on Skills and Job Security

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived impact of AI on the importance of existing skills (enhanced vs. reduced)
notes: Believes AI enhances productivity by automating routine programming tasks
Recognizes that domain knowledge and regulatory expertise become even more important
Feels that communication and critical thinking skills are emphasized as AI handles basic tasks

subtopics.subtopic_description: Emerging skills or new areas of responsibility created by AI
notes: Developing skills in prompt engineering to better utilize LLMs
Increasing focus on AI tool evaluation and validation for clinical data management
Learning to interpret and communicate AI-generated outputs to stakeholders

subtopics.subtopic_description: Level of concern about AI replacing specific tasks or the overall role
notes: Mildly concerned about automation of repetitive programming and data cleaning tasks
Confident that specialized clinical and regulatory knowledge will remain in high demand
Believes overall role will evolve rather than be replaced due to AI

subtopics.subtopic_description: Availability of override mechanisms or manual checks for AI-driven processes
notes: All AI-generated code and documentation are subject to human review before submission
Manual validation steps are built into the data management workflow
Can always revert to manual processes if AI output is unsatisfactory

subtopics.subtopic_description: Perceived change in team or company policies regarding AI adoption
notes: Team is increasingly open to AI adoption, with more discussions about best practices
No formal company-wide AI policy yet, but pilot projects are underway
Policies may soon require documentation of AI involvement in key deliverables

subtopics.subtopic_description: General outlook on AI's broader societal and industry impact
notes: Sees AI as a major driver of efficiency and innovation in clinical research
Believes AI will enable faster and more accurate drug development
Aware of potential risks such as bias and overreliance on automation

subtopics.subtopic_description: Personal beliefs about the ethics and risks of AI in the workplace
notes: Supports responsible AI use with clear guidelines and human oversight
Believes transparency and explainability are critical for ethical AI deployment
Worries about data privacy and the potential for AI to make unintentional errors

subtopics.subtopic_description: Missing AI tools or features that would be most beneficial in the future
notes: Would benefit from AI tools that directly integrate with SAS/R and EDC systems
Desires AI features for automated regulatory compliance checks
Wishes for better AI-powered data visualization and anomaly detection tools

subtopics.subtopic_description: Predicted evolution of their job in the next 5-10 years with AI integration
notes: Anticipates a shift toward more strategic and oversight roles as routine tasks are automated
Expects increased collaboration with AI for data analysis and reporting
Predicts new responsibilities in validating and managing AI-driven processes
Believes regulatory requirements will evolve to address AI's growing role

subtopics.subtopic_description: Concrete steps they would want their organization to take regarding AI strategy
notes: Implement formal AI training programs tailored to clinical data management
Develop clear policies for AI validation, documentation, and accountability
Invest in AI tools that are purpose-built for the clinical research domain
Establish cross-functional AI working groups to share best practices and coordinate adoption
